title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: , estimate logistic regression 1000000 observations 50 covariates. vary number cores used nthreads argument parglm.control. method argument sets method used. LINPACK uses pivoted QR (dqrdc2) glm.fit final decomposition; LAPACK uses DGEQP3 LAPACK throughout; FAST solves normal equations Fisher information, similar speedglm package. plot shows median run times versus number cores. Coloured horizontal lines show single-threaded reference times glm, speedglm, glm2, fastglm, bigglm, bam. used glm.fit parglm.fit. make relative difference bigger call e.g., model.matrix model.frame take time. show point, first compute much time takes make plot. black solid line computation time model.matrix model.frame. Plot runtime versus number cores. open circles FAST method filled circles LINPACK method. FAST method, speedglm, fastglm, fastglm (LDLT) compute Fisher information solve normal equation; fastglm (LDLT) uses LDLT Cholesky decomposition (method 3) rather default column-pivoted QR (method 0). advantage terms computation cost may lead unstable solutions. can alter number observations parallel chunk block_size argument parglm.control. single threaded performance parglm may slower coefficients. cause seems difference LAPACK LINPACK implementation. presumably due either QR decomposition method /qr.qty method. Windows, parglm seem slower built Rtools reason seems qr.qty method LAPACK, dormqr, slower LINPACK method, dqrsl. illustration computation times machine.
code: ##### # simulate n # number of observations #> [1] 1000000 p # number of covariates #> [1] 50 set.seed(68024947) X <- matrix(rnorm(n * p, 1/p, 1/sqrt(p)), n, ncol = p) df <- data.frame(y = 1/(1 + exp(-(rowSums(X) - 1))) > runif(n), X) ##### # compute and measure time. Setup call to make library(microbenchmark) library(speedglm) #> Loading required package: Matrix #> Loading required package: MASS #> Loading required package: biglm #> Loading required package: DBI library(fastglm) library(glm2) #>  #> Attaching package: 'glm2' #> The following object is masked from 'package:MASS': #>  #>     crabs library(biglm) library(mgcv) #> Loading required package: nlme #> This is mgcv 1.9-4. For overview type '?mgcv'. #>  #> Attaching package: 'mgcv' #> The following object is masked from 'package:fastglm': #>  #>     negbin library(parglm) X_mat <- model.matrix(y ~ ., df) biglm_form <- reformulate(names(df)[-1], response = "y") bam_form   <- biglm_form cl <- list(   quote(microbenchmark),   glm      = quote(glm     (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   speedglm = quote(speedglm(y ~ ., family = binomial(), data = df)),   glm2     = quote(glm2    (y ~ ., binomial(), df)),   fastglm  = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial())),   fastglm3 = quote(fastglm (X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial(), method = 3L)),   bigglm   = quote(bigglm  (biglm_form, df, family = binomial())),   bam      = quote(bam     (bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df)),   times = 11L) tfunc <- function(method = "LINPACK", nthreads)   parglm(y ~ ., binomial(), df, control = parglm.control(method = method,                                                          nthreads = nthreads)) cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(nthreads = .(i))))) names(cl)[10:(10L + n_threads - 1L)] <- paste0("parglm-LINPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "FAST")))) names(cl)[(10L + n_threads):(10L + 2L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-FAST-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- c(   cl, lapply(1:n_threads, function(i) bquote(tfunc(     nthreads = .(i), method = "LAPACK")))) names(cl)[(10L + 2L * n_threads):(10L + 3L * n_threads - 1L)] <-   paste0("parglm-LAPACK-", 1:n_threads)  cl <- as.call(cl) cl # the call we make #> microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = speedglm(y ~  #>     ., family = binomial(), data = df), glm2 = glm2(y ~ ., binomial(),  #>     df), fastglm = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial()),  #>     fastglm3 = fastglm(X_mat, as.numeric(df$y), family = binomial(),  #>         method = 3L), bigglm = bigglm(biglm_form, df, family = binomial()),  #>     bam = bam(bam_form, family = binomial(), data = df), times = 11L,  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L), `parglm-LINPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L), `parglm-LINPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L), `parglm-LINPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L), `parglm-LINPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L),  #>     `parglm-LINPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L), `parglm-LINPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L),  #>     `parglm-FAST-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-FAST-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L, method = "FAST"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-1` = tfunc(nthreads = 1L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-2` = tfunc(nthreads = 2L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-3` = tfunc(nthreads = 3L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-4` = tfunc(nthreads = 4L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-5` = tfunc(nthreads = 5L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-6` = tfunc(nthreads = 6L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-7` = tfunc(nthreads = 7L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-8` = tfunc(nthreads = 8L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-9` = tfunc(nthreads = 9L, method = "LAPACK"),  #>     `parglm-LAPACK-10` = tfunc(nthreads = 10L, method = "LAPACK"))  out <- eval(cl) #> Warning in microbenchmark(glm = glm(y ~ ., binomial(), df), speedglm = #> speedglm(y ~ : less accurate nanosecond times to avoid potential integer #> overflows s <- summary(out) # result from `microbenchmark` print(s[, c("expr", "min", "mean", "median", "max")], digits  = 3,       row.names = FALSE) #>               expr  min mean median  max #>                glm 2404 2480   2460 2663 #>           speedglm  725  787    761 1065 #>               glm2 2427 2526   2482 2976 #>            fastglm 1895 1992   1940 2162 #>           fastglm3  547  558    555  585 #>             bigglm 6173 6416   6379 7011 #>                bam 4906 5067   5055 5340 #>   parglm-LINPACK-1 1825 1879   1870 1963 #>   parglm-LINPACK-2 1646 1704   1676 1832 #>   parglm-LINPACK-3 1569 1608   1607 1648 #>   parglm-LINPACK-4 1560 1611   1605 1743 #>   parglm-LINPACK-5 1397 1439   1427 1537 #>   parglm-LINPACK-6 1398 1421   1412 1471 #>   parglm-LINPACK-7 1379 1473   1419 1713 #>   parglm-LINPACK-8 1356 1457   1406 1846 #>   parglm-LINPACK-9 1382 1418   1413 1465 #>  parglm-LINPACK-10 1347 1372   1374 1393 #>      parglm-FAST-1  449  510    491  592 #>      parglm-FAST-2  387  414    408  488 #>      parglm-FAST-3  372  414    414  460 #>      parglm-FAST-4  367  423    405  527 #>      parglm-FAST-5  381  431    387  675 #>      parglm-FAST-6  348  413    395  584 #>      parglm-FAST-7  347  374    368  406 #>      parglm-FAST-8  348  393    381  454 #>      parglm-FAST-9  348  407    387  556 #>     parglm-FAST-10  338  373    372  440 #>    parglm-LAPACK-1 1825 1885   1873 2058 #>    parglm-LAPACK-2 1646 1670   1665 1733 #>    parglm-LAPACK-3 1586 1617   1609 1685 #>    parglm-LAPACK-4 1557 1598   1588 1676 #>    parglm-LAPACK-5 1390 1444   1440 1580 #>    parglm-LAPACK-6 1378 1399   1391 1451 #>    parglm-LAPACK-7 1368 1411   1403 1489 #>    parglm-LAPACK-8 1376 1403   1396 1475 #>    parglm-LAPACK-9 1365 1420   1400 1597 #>   parglm-LAPACK-10 1363 1413   1401 1507 modmat_time <- microbenchmark(   modmat_time = {     mf <- model.frame(y ~ ., df); model.matrix(terms(mf), mf)   }, times = 10) modmat_time # time taken by `model.matrix` and `model.frame` #> Unit: milliseconds #>         expr       min        lq       mean    median         uq        max #>  modmat_time 75.486453 93.180659 98.3097262 95.229306 112.323805 121.203257 #>  neval #>     10 par(mar = c(4.5, 4.5, .5, 9)) o <- aggregate(time ~ expr, out, median)[, 2] / 10^9 ylim <- range(o, 0); ylim[2] <- ylim[2] + .04 * diff(ylim)  o_linpack <- o[8L:(n_threads + 7L)] o_fast    <- o[(n_threads + 8L):(2L * n_threads + 7L)] o_lapack  <- o[(2L * n_threads + 8L):(3L * n_threads + 7L)]  ref_cols <- c("#E69F00", "#56B4E9", "#009E73", "#0072B2", "#F0E442", "#D55E00", "#CC79A7") ref_lty  <- c("dashed", "dotted", "dotdash", "longdash", "44", "twodash", "1373")  plot(1:n_threads, o_linpack, xlab = "Number of cores", yaxs = "i",      ylim = ylim, ylab = "Run time (s)", pch = 16, cex = 1.4) points(1:n_threads, o_fast,   pch = 1, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) points(1:n_threads, o_lapack, pch = 4, cex = 1.4, lwd = 2) for (i in 1:7)   abline(h = o[i], lty = ref_lty[i], col = ref_cols[i], lwd = 2) abline(h = median(modmat_time$time) / 10^9, lty = "solid", col = "black",        lwd = 2) usr <- par("usr") par(xpd = TRUE) legend(x = usr[2], y = usr[4], xjust = 0, yjust = 1,        legend = c("glm", "speedglm", "glm2", "fastglm", "fastglm (LDLT)",                   "bigglm", "bam", "model.matrix", "parglm LINPACK",                   "parglm FAST", "parglm LAPACK"),        lty = c(ref_lty, "solid", NA, NA, NA),        lwd = c(2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, 2, NA, NA, NA),        col = c(ref_cols, "black", "black", "black", "black"),        pch = c(NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, NA, 16, 1, 4),        bty = "n") qr1 <- qr(X) qr2 <- qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE) microbenchmark::microbenchmark(   `qr LINPACK`     = qr(X),   `qr LAPACK`      = qr(X, LAPACK = TRUE),   `qr.qty LINPACK` = qr.qty(qr1, df$y),   `qr.qty LAPACK`  = qr.qty(qr2, df$y),   times = 11) #> Unit: milliseconds #>            expr        min          lq        mean     median          uq #>      qr LINPACK 405.653672 417.4386915 423.2286798 418.273882 419.5840165 #>       qr LAPACK 366.584813 368.9630590 378.3611275 371.411456 381.3625660 #>  qr.qty LINPACK  45.758993  49.6602865  97.6999660  56.960808  84.8598935 #>   qr.qty LAPACK 110.668717 110.8921875 116.2642069 111.939102 114.2420515 #>         max neval #>  474.846625    11 #>  403.544468    11 #>  401.498322    11 #>  155.756827    11

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: smaller datasets per-call overhead parglm’s thread pool can exceed gains parallelism. repeat benchmark n = 100,000 n = 10,000, dropping bam since aimed much larger problems. Note default block_size parglm.control 10000, n = 10,000 work split across threads parglm effectively runs single-threaded regardless nthreads.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 10,000.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: per-row cost method scales roughly \(p^2\) (computing \(X'WX\) IRLS iteration), parglm’s thread-pool overhead roughly fixed per call. two effects compete: small \(p\) overhead dominates parallel benefit, larger \(p\) parallel work amortizes overhead parglm pulls ahead. contrast n = 100,000, p = 5 (overhead wins) n = 1,000,000, p = 20 (parallel work wins).

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 100,000 p = 5.

title: Introduction to the parglm package
text: Plot runtime versus number cores n = 1,000,000 p = 20.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: simulate Poisson dataset 20 clusters 10 observations , cluster-level random effect induces within-cluster correlation.
code: library(parglm) library(sandwich) library(lmtest) set.seed(1) n          <- 200 cluster_id <- rep(1:20, each = 10) x1         <- rnorm(n) x2         <- rnorm(n) u          <- rep(rnorm(20, sd = 0.5), each = 10)  # cluster random effect y          <- rpois(n, exp(0.5 + 0.3 * x1 - 0.2 * x2 + u)) dat        <- data.frame(y = y, x1 = x1, x2 = x2, cluster = cluster_id)

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
code: fit <- parglm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat, family = poisson(),               control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: default model-based standard errors assume Poisson variance equals mean. small cluster random effects induce overdispersion.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: vcovHC() computes sandwich standard errors robust misspecification variance function. HC3 (default) recommended small moderate samples.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: vcovCL() accounts within-cluster correlation, appropriate correction . expected, cluster-robust standard errors larger model-based ones, reflecting extra variability due cluster random effects.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: covariance matrices also available:

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: model = TRUE (default parglm) must set model frame stored, allowing sandwich reconstruct design matrix internally.

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: gtsummary package produces publication-ready regression tables model objects. parglm() models supported directly inherit glm.
code: library(gtsummary)

title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: fit logistic regression model parglm() pass tbl_regression(). Setting exponentiate = TRUE displays odds ratios confidence intervals.
code: set.seed(2) n2    <- 300 x1_b  <- rnorm(n2) x2_b  <- rnorm(n2) y_bin <- rbinom(n2, 1, plogis(0.4 + 0.6 * x1_b - 0.4 * x2_b)) dat_b <- data.frame(y = y_bin, x1 = x1_b, x2 = x2_b)  fit_logistic <- parglm(y ~ x1 + x2, data = dat_b, family = binomial(),                        control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  suppressWarnings(   tbl_regression(fit_logistic, exponentiate = TRUE) )

what: Robust standard errors in a gtsummary table
title: Robust standard errors with parglm and the sandwich package and regression tables with gtsummary
text: tidy_parglm_robust() drop-tidy_fun tbl_regression() replaces default model-based standard errors sandwich estimates. use cluster-robust standard errors Poisson model fitted earlier.
code: tbl_regression(fit, tidy_fun = tidy_parglm_robust)

title: Parallel GLM
text: parglm package provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS. can install release version CRAN install development version GitHub calling: See vignette example run times details.
code: install.packages("parglm") remotes::install_github("remlapmot/parglm")

title: parglm: Parallel GLM — parglm-package
text: Provides parallel estimation method generalized linear models without compiling multithreaded LAPACK BLAS.

what: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Auxiliary function parglm fitting.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: parglm.control(   epsilon = 1e-08,   maxit = 25,   trace = FALSE,   nthreads = parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L),   block_size = NULL,   method = "LINPACK",   nthreads_auto = missing(nthreads) )

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: epsilon positive convergence tolerance. maxit integer giving maximal number IWLS iterations. trace logical indicating output produced estimation. nthreads number cores use. Defaults parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L), leaves one core free. may get best performance using available physical cores data set sufficiently large. block_size number observations include parallel block. method string specifying method use. Either "LINPACK", "LAPACK", "FAST". nthreads_auto logical; internal use . Records whether nthreads auto-detected (suppresses thread-reduction warning dataset small). set argument directly.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: list components named arguments.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: LINPACK method uses QR method glm.fit final QR decomposition. dqrdc2 method described qr. QR decompositions last made DGEQP3 LAPACK. See Wood, Goude, Shaw (2015) details QR method. FAST method computes Fisher information solves normal equation. faster less numerically stable.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
text: Wood, S.N., Goude, Y. & Shaw S. (2015) Generalized additive models large datasets. Journal Royal Statistical Society, Series C 64(1): 139-155.

title: Auxiliary for Controlling GLM Fitting in Parallel — parglm.control
code: # use one core f1 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L))  # use two cores (mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: Function like glm can make computation parallel. function supports families listed family. See "vignette("parglm", "parglm")" run time examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: parglm(   formula,   family = gaussian,   data,   weights,   subset,   na.action,   start = NULL,   offset,   control = list(...),   contrasts = NULL,   model = TRUE,   x = FALSE,   y = TRUE,   ... )  parglm.fit(   x,   y,   weights = rep(1, NROW(x)),   start = NULL,   etastart = NULL,   mustart = NULL,   offset = rep(0, NROW(x)),   family = gaussian(),   control = list(),   intercept = TRUE,   ... )

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: formula object class formula. family family object. data optional data frame, list environment containing variables model. weights optional vector 'prior weights' used fitting process. NULL numeric vector. subset optional vector specifying subset observations used fitting process. na.action function indicates happen data contain NAs. start starting values parameters linear predictor. offset can used specify priori known component included linear predictor fitting. control list parameters controlling fitting process. parglm.fit passed parglm.control. contrasts optional list. See contrasts.arg model.matrix.default. model logical value indicating whether model frame included component returned value. x, y parglm: logical values indicating whether response vector model matrix used fitting process returned components returned value. parglm.fit: x design matrix dimension n * p, y vector observations length n. ... parglm: arguments used form default control argument supplied directly. parglm.fit: unused. etastart starting values linear predictor. supported. mustart starting values vector means. supported. intercept logical. intercept included null model?

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: glm object returned glm differs mainly qr element. qr element object returned parglm(.fit) \(R\) matrix QR decomposition.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
text: current implementation uses min(.integer(n / p), nthreads) threads n number observations, p number covariates, nthreads nthreads element list returned parglm.control. Thus, likely little () reduction computation time p almost equal n. current implementation handle p > n. Since parglm returns standard glm object, compatible sandwich package heteroskedasticity-consistent (HC) cluster-robust standard errors via vcovHC vcovCL. requires model = TRUE (default). See vignette("sandwich", "parglm") examples.

title: Fitting Generalized Linear Models in Parallel — parglm
code: # mtcars has 32 rows, sufficient for 2 threads (>= 16 rows per thread) f1 <- glm   (mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log")) f2 <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars, family = Gamma(link = "log"),              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 2L)) all.equal(coef(f1), coef(f2)) #> [1] TRUE

what: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
text: drop-tidy_fun tbl_regression computes heteroskedasticity-consistent (HC) cluster-robust confidence intervals via sandwich lmtest.

title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
code: tidy_parglm_robust(   x,   vcov. = "HC3",   conf.int = TRUE,   conf.level = 0.95,   exponentiate = FALSE,   ... )

title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
text: x parglm (glm) model object. vcov. robust variance-covariance estimator. string passed type argument vcovHC (e.g. "HC3"). function called vcov.(x) return covariance matrix (use cluster-robust SEs via vcovCL). matrix used directly. Defaults "HC3". conf.int logical; whether include confidence intervals. conf.level confidence level intervals. exponentiate logical; whether exponentiate estimate confidence interval limits. ... unused; present compatibility tidy_fun interface tbl_regression.

title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
text: data.frame columns term, estimate,   std.error, statistic, p.value, (  conf.int = TRUE) conf.low conf.high.

title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
text: Pass function tidy_fun tbl_regression:
code: # HC3 (default) tbl_regression(fit, tidy_fun = tidy_parglm_robust)  # HC1 tbl_regression(fit, tidy_fun = \(x, ...) tidy_parglm_robust(x, vcov. = "HC1", ...))  # Cluster-robust tbl_regression(fit, tidy_fun = \(x, ...) tidy_parglm_robust(   x, vcov. = \(m) sandwich::vcovCL(m, cluster = ~ cluster_var), ...))

title: Tidy a parglm model with robust standard errors — tidy_parglm_robust
code: fp <- parglm(mpg ~ wt + hp, data = mtcars,              control = parglm.control(nthreads = 1L)) if (requireNamespace("sandwich", quietly = TRUE) &&     requireNamespace("lmtest",   quietly = TRUE)) {   tidy_parglm_robust(fp) } #>                    term    estimate   std.error statistic      p.value #> (Intercept) (Intercept) 37.22727012 2.229805403 16.695300 1.418047e-62 #> wt                   wt -3.87783074 0.768519050 -5.045849 4.515129e-07 #> hp                   hp -0.03177295 0.009385138 -3.385453 7.106078e-04 #>                conf.low   conf.high #> (Intercept) 32.85693183 41.59760840 #> wt          -5.38410040 -2.37156108 #> hp          -0.05016748 -0.01337841

title: parglm 0.1.9
text: CRAN release: 2026-05-12 Add biglm, fastglm, glm2, mgcv timing comparison parglm.Rmd vignette. add timings fewer observations fewer coefficients. Bump roxygen2 8.0.0 Remove busy-wait polling qr_parallel::get_stacks_res Return eta mu parallelglm skip R-side recomputation Sum upper triangle per-chunk Fisher information FAST path Drop const R_F members enable move semantics Hoist NA-zeroing beta per-chunk workers Use thread_local index thread_pool::get_id Skip intermediate matrix reverse-pivoting R Cache parglm_supported keys avoid rebuilding family objects per fit Change default parglm.control(nthreads) parallelly::availableCores(omit = 1L) make better use available cores Replace linked-list task queue deque single mutex Fuse X chunk copy weight scaling one memory pass Support quasibinomial() quasipoisson() family specification Enable full compatibility sandwich package robust standard errors, including adding comments parglm helpfile, new vignette, tests Add fastglm’s method = 3L, parglm’s parglm.control(method = "LAPACK") benchmarks parglm.Rmd vignette Allow two column response family binomial quasibinomial Fix issue order summary.parglm vcov.parglm Warn rather error starting values found Add confint.parglm fix profile Wald CI compatibility, give compatibility gtsummary package (e.g., gtsummary::tbl_regression()) Add tidy_parglm_robust tidying parglm model robust standard errors Add gtsummary examples second vignette

title: parglm 0.1.8
